cot_content:
Okay, so I need to figure out which answer is correct for telling right and left earbuds apart without looking. The options are A and B, both involving tying a knot on the left one, but at different ends.

First, I remember that earbuds usually have a left and right speaker. The left earbud (L) goes in the left ear, and the right (R) in the right. To tell them apart without looking, you'd want a mark that's easy to feel by touch. 

If the knot is tied at the connecting end (the part that connects to the headphone cord or device), which is option A, then when you hold the earbud, you can feel the knot on the left side. But the problem is that the connecting end is the part that's attached to the cord, so tying a knot there might interfere with the connection or be hard to feel once the cord is in use.

Option B suggests tying the knot at the speaker end, which is the part that goes into the ear. If you tie a small knot there, it would create a small bump that you can feel when you pick up the earbud. The left earbud would have this knot, so when you're putting them in, you can feel which one is left by the knot on the speaker end. That seems more practical because you can easily feel the bump when handling the earbuds.

I've seen people do this with earbuds. They might put a small piece of tape, a knot, or a different color on one side. The key is that the mark should be on the end you can easily feel when you're using them. Since the speaker end is the part you handle when inserting, a knot there would be more noticeable. The connecting end is usually part of the cable, so a knot there might not be as helpful once the cable is in your ears or around your neck.

So, the answer is B. Tying the knot at the speaker end of the left earbud allows you to distinguish it by touch when you need to insert it into your ear.
label: majority
